God's holiness—attested in Isaiah's vision of the seraphim crying "holy, holy, holy"—is cited as one of several scriptural witnesses establishing that the word "hallowed" in the Lord's Prayer calls believers to approach God with an attitude of reverence.

context: ...who hath believed our report, and to whom is the arm of the Lord revealed? For he shall grow up before him as a tender plant and as a root out of a dry ground...

context: Thus saith the Lord, Set thine house

context: he's reading Isaiah 53

context: in the year that king Uzziah died i saw also the lord sitting upon a throne high and lifted up and his train filled the temple
point_summary: Isaiah's vision of the enthroned, thrice-holy God is presented as the necessary corrective to a diminished view of God, because seeing God in His rightful place simultaneously reveals human unworthiness and humbles pride.

context: above it stood the seraphims each one had six wings with twain he covered his face and with twain he covered his feet and with twain he did fly
point_summary: The seraphim's posture of reverence in Isaiah 6:2 is cited within a vision of God "high and lifted up" to argue that a right view of God's holiness necessarily produces humility and a right view of oneself.

context: and one cried unto another and said holy holy holy is the lord of hosts the whole earth full of his glory
point_summary: The seraphim's threefold "holy" declaration reveals God as high, holy, and lifted up — a vision that, when truly seen, humbles the viewer by showing both God and humanity in their rightful places.

context: the post of the door moved at the voice of him that cried and that house was filled with smoke
point_summary: Isaiah 6:4's depiction of the shaking thresholds and smoke-filled temple frames the broader vision of God "high and lifted up," used here to argue that a right view of God's holiness produces humility and self-abasement in the one who sees Him.

context: woe is me, woe is me, for I am undone because I am a man of unclean lips and I dwell in the midst of a people of unclean lips, for mine eyes have seen the King, the Lord of hosts.
point_summary: A vision of God in his rightful place as the holy, transcendent Creator produces immediate self-awareness of unworthiness—the surest remedy for pride—and prepares the one who sees him for ambassadorial sending.

context: Then flew one of the seraphims unto me, having a live coal in his hand, which he had taken with the tongs from off the altar,
point_summary: Seeing God in his rightful place—high, holy, and glorious—simultaneously exposes human unworthiness and, after cleansing, produces the readiness to answer God's call for faithful ambassadors.

context: and he laid it upon my mouth and said, Lo, this hath touched thy lips, and thine iniquity is taken away, and thy sin purged.
point_summary: Seeing God in his rightful place—holy and exalted—produces the humility and cleansing that precede Isaiah's commissioning, establishing the pattern for faithful ambassadors who are sent only after being undone and purged.

context: Isaiah responds with those words, Here am I, send me.
point_summary: Isaiah's immediate, unconditional surrender — "Here am I, send me" — before knowing the task illustrates that a vision of God in His rightful place is sufficient motivation for faithful ambassadorship regardless of outcome.

context: The Bible says in Isaiah 64 that all of our good works are as filthy rags

context: all of our good works are as filthy rags

context: in Isaiah 40 we read about that physical strength being renewed

context: but they that wait upon the Lord shall renew their strength they shall mount up with wings as eagles they shall run and not be weary and they shall walk and not faint
point_summary: Those who wait on the Lord receive renewed strength for whatever He has called them to do, overcoming human weariness through dependence on Him rather than their own capacity.

context: the promise that God gave, that his word would not return void

context: Isaiah seven says this

context: chapter number nine

context: the Lord himself shall give you a sign. Behold, a virgin shall conceive and bear a son

context: and shall call his name Emmanuel
point_summary: Isaiah 7:14's specific word "virgin" is defended as theologically essential — not interchangeable with "young maid" — because the virgin birth was necessary to ensure Christ was born without a sin nature.

context: for unto us a child is born, unto us a son is given and the government shall be upon his shoulder and his name shall be called Wonderful, Counselor, the Mighty God, the Everlasting Father, the Prince of Peace
point_summary: The names given to the promised child in Isaiah 9:6 — Wonderful, Counselor, Mighty God, Everlasting Father, Prince of Peace — are presented alongside verse 7 as a proclamation of Messiah's birth and the coming fulfillment of the Davidic covenant in Christ's millennial reign.

context: these prophecies of Isaiah seven and Isaiah nine
point_summary: The virgin birth prophesied in Isaiah 7 was theologically necessary because Christ had to be born without a sin nature, distinguishing "virgin" from merely "young maid."

context: these prophecies of Isaiah seven and Isaiah nine

context: the fulfillment of what we had read there in Isaiah chapter number nine

context: If you understand and you read over in Isaiah, the Bible talks about the evil, the wicked, they have no peace, they have no peace
point_summary: Isaiah's declaration that the wicked have no peace is cited to contrast the false, fragile peace the world pursues with the true peace that comes only from God.

context: for unto us a child is born, unto us a son is given, and the government shall be upon his shoulder
point_summary: Christ's identity as "the root of David" in Revelation 5:5 is grounded in Isaiah 9:6–7, which prophesies an eternal reign established on David's throne by the one born as the Mighty God and Prince of Peace.

context: Isaiah 40 and verse number 18, to whom then will you liken God or what likeness will you compare unto Him?

context: that bringeth the princes to nothing. He maketh the judges of the earth as vanity

context: Lift up your eyes on high and behold who hath created these things that bringeth out their hosts by number. He calleth them all by names

context: There is no searching of His understanding.

context: there's none like unto thee

point_summary: Hezekiah's crisis under Assyrian threat is introduced as a biblical example of what it means to bring an impossible problem before Elohim, the creator God who can do all things.

context: The Bible says in verse 15 of Isaiah 37, and Hezekiah prayed unto the Lord, saying
point_summary: Hezekiah's prayer to Jehovah in Isaiah 37:15 introduces a sermon illustration connecting God's covenant name to His power as Elohim, the strong Creator, against whom no enemy's threats can stand.

context: O Lord of hosts, by the way, another name that you can look at, you can understand, we'll deal with that later, he says, God of Israel, that dwellest between the cherubims, thou art the God, even thou alone, of all the kingdoms of the earth, thou hast made heaven and earth
point_summary: Hezekiah's prayer grounds his appeal for deliverance in God's identity as Creator of heaven and earth, distinguishing the living God from the idols of conquered nations, which were merely the work of men's hands.

context: He is the Prince of Peace
point_summary: God is identified as the Prince of Peace—the title drawn from Isaiah 9:6—as the basis for the assurance that He can grant peace to any believer who, like David, turns directly to Him.

context: the Bible says in Isaiah 48 in verse 22, there is no peace, saith the Lord, unto the wicked
point_summary: No peace exists for the wicked, establishing that apart from salvation humanity universally lacks the peace that only God provides.

context: And we see that repeated again in Isaiah 57
point_summary: The absence of peace for the wicked, declared in Isaiah 48:22 and repeated in Isaiah 57, is applied to the universal condition of lost humanity before salvation.

context: I mentioned Isaiah 48
point_summary: Isaiah 48:22's declaration that "there is no peace unto the wicked" grounds the sermon's argument that lost humanity universally lacks peace apart from salvation through Christ.

context: For unto us a child is born, unto us a son is given, and the government shall be upon his shoulder, and his name shall be called Wonderful, Counselor, the mighty God, the everlasting Father, the Prince of Peace
point_summary: Isaiah 9:6's messianic title "Prince of Peace" is presented as the announcement of peace to all humanity, paralleling the personal announcement of peace Jehovah spoke to Gideon.

context: he shall be called Wonderful, Counselor, the mighty God, the everlasting Father, the Prince of Peace
point_summary: Isaiah 9:6's messianic title "Prince of Peace" is read alongside the angelic announcement in Luke 2 as God's declaration to all humanity that peace would come through the incarnate Christ.

context: that Messianic prophecy in the book of Isaiah, and to understand that Messiah, Jesus was gonna come
point_summary: The Messianic title "Prince of Peace" in Isaiah is read as prophetic anticipation of the one through whom alone, according to Romans 5, sinners can have peace with God.

context: the Prince of Peace
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: their heart was far from God
point_summary: Forgetting God, as warned in Scripture, is not an academic lapse but a practical one—going through religious motions while the heart remains far from him, chasing other things.

context: all of our righteousness are as filthy rags
point_summary: Human righteousness being utterly insufficient is established by Isaiah 64:6's "filthy rags" language, which the Holy Ghost uses to convince the lost that Christ's righteousness alone must be imputed to their account.

context: They don't see Him as Isaiah saw Him in Isaiah 6, where His train did fill the temple. Where Isaiah is broken because of his own sin, and he says, woe is me for I'm unclean.
point_summary: God's holiness demands that He be approached on His own terms, as Isaiah's vision of the Lord "high, holy, and lifted up" left him undone—a standard the sermon contrasts with contemporary, man-determined worship.

context: individuals were calling good evil and evil good
point_summary: Cultural and societal rejection of God's law—calling good evil and evil good—is cited as evidence that "they have made void thy law," mirroring the psalmist's recognition of peril.

context: God's ways are not our ways
point_summary: God's ways transcend human comprehension, illustrated by Gideon's army being reduced to an impossibly small number so that only God could receive credit for the victory.

context: therefore the Lord Himself shall give you a sign
point_summary: Isaiah 7:14's prophecy of a virgin-born son named Immanuel is presented as the direct fulfillment of the "her seed" promise first announced in Genesis 3:15, tracing the virgin birth through Scripture.

context: Behold, a virgin shall conceive and bear a son and shall call His name Immanuel
point_summary: Isaiah 7:14 is read as the fulfillment of Genesis 3:15's "her seed," confirming the virgin birth ensured Christ's sinless incarnation by bypassing the sin nature transmitted through Adam.

context: we just read this in Isaiah seven and verse number 14, behold, a virgin shall be with child and shall bring forth a son, and they shall call his name Emmanuel, which being interpreted is God with us
point_summary: Matthew 1:22–23 presents Isaiah 7:14 as explicitly fulfilled in the virgin birth of Christ, connecting it to the proto-evangelium of Genesis 3:15 as part of God's redemptive promise.
